text: To wash your hands properly, first remove rings or other jewellery from your hands and wrists. Wet your hands with running water, then wash all parts of your hands with plain soap for at least 20 seconds, rubbing your hands together to create lather. Rinse your hands well under running water, then dry them with a clean cloth or paper towel.

source.title: Hand washing: Help stop the spread of germs (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #85, section "How do I properly wash my hands?" (last updated 2025-04-09)

text: Wash your hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds. In a public restroom, use the towel to turn off the tap and to open the door when you leave so your clean hands do not pick up germs again.

source.title: Hand washing: Help stop the spread of germs (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #85, section "How do I properly wash my hands?" (last updated 2025-04-09)

text: If soap and water are not available, use an alcohol-based hand sanitizer and rub your hands together until they are dry. Use a sanitizer only when your hands are not visibly soiled. Keep sanitizers out of the reach of children because they may be harmful if swallowed.

source.title: Hand washing: Help stop the spread of germs (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #85, section "How do I properly wash my hands?" (last updated 2025-04-09)

text: Take your medicines exactly as your doctor prescribed, including what time of day you take them and how much you take. Try to take each of your medicines at the same time each day.

source.title: Taking Medicines as Prescribed (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How to Take Your Medicines"

text: To keep track of all your pills, try using a weekly medicine organizer. Make a list of your medicines and post it somewhere you will see it often.

source.title: Taking Medicines as Prescribed (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How to Take Your Medicines"

text: Do not use less of your medicine, such as taking half a dose or using it every other day, because the medicine will not work the way it should. Using more than one drugstore can cause problems, because unless you tell them, the pharmacists may not know all the medicines you take.

source.title: Taking Medicines as Prescribed (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Overcoming Barriers" / Overview

text: Wash your hands before you eat or handle any food, and after you use the bathroom. Scrub all parts of your hands with soap for at least 20 seconds. Wash plates, utensils, cutting boards and countertops with hot soapy water.

source.title: Food safety: Easy ways to make food safer (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #59a, section "Clean" (last updated 2023-03-10)

text: Separate raw meat, poultry and seafood from other foods. Use one cutting board for raw meats and a different one for ready-to-eat foods.

source.title: Food safety: Easy ways to make food safer (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #59a, section "Separate" (last updated 2023-03-10)

text: Cooking foods to an internal temperature of 74°C (165°F) or hotter kills germs. Reheat leftovers to at least 74°C (165°F).

source.title: Food safety: Easy ways to make food safer (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #59a, section "Cook" (last updated 2023-03-10)

text: Do not keep meat, poultry, fish, dairy or cooked leftovers at room temperature for more than 2 hours. Refrigerate leftovers within two hours of preparing. Keep your fridge set at 4°C (40°F) or lower. Germs grow rapidly at temperatures between 4°C and 60°C; this is called the "danger zone".

source.title: Food safety: Easy ways to make food safer (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #59a, section "Chill" (last updated 2023-03-10)

text: Brush twice daily with a soft-bristled toothbrush. Place the brush at a 45-degree angle where the teeth meet the gums. Press firmly and gently rock the brush back and forth using small circular movements. Avoid vigorous scrubbing, as it can damage gums and tooth enamel.

source.title: Dental Care: Brushing and Flossing Your Teeth (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How to Brush Your Teeth"

text: Brush chewing surfaces vigorously with short back-and-forth strokes. Use a fluoride-containing toothpaste. Consider using disclosing tablets occasionally to identify any plaque you may have missed.

source.title: Dental Care: Brushing and Flossing Your Teeth (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How to Brush Your Teeth"

text: Floss at least once a day. Work the floss gently toward the gums, curve it around each tooth into a U-shape, and move it firmly up and down several times to scrape off plaque.

source.title: Dental Care: Brushing and Flossing Your Teeth (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How to Floss Your Teeth"

text: Use the back of your hand to test the water to make sure it is not too hot or cold. Gather materials including face cloths, towels, and bar or liquid soap before beginning. Allow the person to bathe themselves as much as possible.

source.title: Caregiving: How to Help With a Sink Bath (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Preparing for a sink bath"

text: Use a bath seat so you can take a shower or bath sitting down. Install grab bars by the toilet, in bathtub and shower areas. Use only non-slip rugs and non-slip mats in hallways and bathrooms. Wipe up moisture or spills right away.

source.title: Seniors' falls can be prevented (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #78, section "How can falls be prevented?" (last updated July 31, 2024)

text: Handrails and a non-skid mat inside and outside the shower or tub can help prevent falls. Before the shower, gather supplies including face cloths, towels, soap, and tear-free or no-rinse shampoo. Test water temperature using the back of your hand.

source.title: Caregiving: How to Help With a Shower (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Safety Equipment" and "Preparing for a Shower"

text: Provide a soaped face cloth and wash as much as possible independently. Assist with hard-to-reach areas like the back. Use a removable shower head for rinsing. Allow standing water to drain before stepping out, then dry difficult areas with a towel and apply body lotion to appropriate areas.

source.title: Caregiving: How to Help With a Shower (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Shower Steps"

text: Examine skin during bathing for signs of rashes or sores, paying special attention to creased areas and bony prominences. Avoid massaging reddened areas, as this can cause tissue damage.

source.title: Caregiving: How to Help With a Shower (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Important Monitoring"

text: Wash your face once or twice a day with warm water and a mild soap or acne wash. Use gentle, circling motions rather than scrubbing your face.

source.title: Quick Tips: Keeping Acne Under Control (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Daily tips for controlling acne"

text: Always completely rinse your skin after you wash it, and gently pat it dry. Avoid rubbing your skin. If washing causes dryness, apply moisturizing lotion labelled "non-comedogenic."

source.title: Quick Tips: Keeping Acne Under Control (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Daily tips for controlling acne"

text: Shower as soon as you can after any activity that causes heavy sweating, especially sports. Try not to scrub or pick at your skin, as this can make things worse and cause scars.

source.title: Quick Tips: Keeping Acne Under Control (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Daily tips for controlling acne"

text: Take your dentures out of your mouth and rinse them to remove any loose food. Use a brush designed for cleaning dentures, or use a toothbrush with soft bristles. Gently brush every surface of your dentures with a denture cleanser such as Efferdent or Polident. Do not use toothpaste or a brush with hard bristles — they can scratch the dentures.

source.title: Dental Care for Older Adults (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Caring for your dentures"

text: Store dentures in lukewarm water or denture-cleaning liquid overnight. Do not put them in hot water, and do not let them dry out. Stand over a folded towel or bowl of water when taking the dentures out, in case they are dropped.

source.title: Dental Care for Older Adults (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Caring for your dentures"

text: Leave your dentures out at least 6 hours at night — this gives your gums a chance to rest. Brush your gums, your tongue, and the roof of your mouth with a soft-bristled brush. If your gums are red or swollen, let them heal before putting your dentures in again.

source.title: Dental Care for Older Adults (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Caring for your dentures"

text: Wash and dry your hands before beginning. Hold the hand steady while trimming nails straight across. Keep nails even with — or not much longer than — the tip of the finger. Trim nails weekly, ideally after showering, as moisture softens nails.

source.title: Caregiving: Fingernail Care (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Trimming Fingernails"

text: Use a nail file to gently smooth the edges of the nails, especially at the corners. Never trim or cut the cuticles — even a minor cut risks infection. Watch for signs of infection such as pain, swelling, redness, or warmth, especially in people with diabetes.

source.title: Caregiving: Fingernail Care (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Trimming Fingernails"

text: When assisting with hand hygiene, use a nail brush to clean underneath nails. Nails tend to get harder with age and the skin on the hands can become thin and dry, so offering hand lotion afterward is beneficial.

source.title: Caregiving: Fingernail Care (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "General Nail Care"

text: Supplies needed include towels, shampoo, conditioner, hair-washing trays or basins, combs, and styling tools. Drape a towel or cape over the shoulders. Pour warm water over the hair using a container or hose attachment to wet it thoroughly.

source.title: Caregiving: Washing and Styling Hair (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Getting Ready" and "Washing Hair in a Chair at Sink"

text: Apply no-tears shampoo starting at the hairline at the forehead, then move to the sides and back. Gently lift the head to reach the back of the head and neck. Rinse, apply conditioner if needed, then rinse again.

source.title: Caregiving: Washing and Styling Hair (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Washing Hair in a Chair at Sink"

text: After rinsing, dry hair with a towel or hair dryer. Comb and style as preferred. If water washing is not possible, use a dry shampoo, a no-rinse shampoo cap, or a no-rinse shampoo as alternatives.

source.title: Caregiving: Washing and Styling Hair (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Washing Hair in a Chair at Sink"

text: Check your feet daily for blisters, cuts, cracks, sores, broken skin, corns, calluses, or ingrown toenails. Diabetes damages nerves and blood vessels in the feet, making you less likely to notice injuries. A minor foot injury could become an ulcer or a serious infection.

source.title: Diabetes: Taking Care of Your Feet (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How do you care for your feet?"

text: Wash your feet every day using warm (not hot) water and dry them thoroughly, especially between the toes. Apply lotion to your feet but avoid the area between the toes.

source.title: Diabetes: Taking Care of Your Feet (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How do you care for your feet?"

text: Wear well-fitting shoes with arch support, adequate toe space, and seamless socks. Inspect shoes daily for debris before putting them on. Avoid going barefoot. Have a doctor check your feet at each visit and obtain a complete foot exam annually through a doctor or podiatrist.

source.title: Diabetes: Taking Care of Your Feet (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How do you care for your feet?"

text: Use a plastic pillbox with dividers for each day's medicines. Choose the appropriate size based on your medication frequency—small boxes with a few compartments work for once-daily medicines, while larger ones suit multiple daily doses. Position the pillbox where you will see it before meals or at the time you take your medicines. If the end of the day comes and a compartment still has a pill in it, you likely forgot to take that dose.

source.title: Keeping Track of Medicines (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Using a Pillbox" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Keep at least one original pill bottle alongside the pillbox. That way, if you forget what a pill is for, you can check the bottle it came from. Avoid using a pillbox for medicines that must stay in their original containers to remain stable or effective.

source.title: Keeping Track of Medicines (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Using a Pillbox" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: A pillbox is a recommended basic home health item. Be sure to leave at least one pill in the original bottle so that if you forget what a pill is for, you can find it in the bottle it came from. Certain medications require original container storage to prevent spoilage. Store all medicines and natural health products as directed and keep them out of reach of children.

source.title: Your Home Health Centre (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Medicine Storage and Safety Guidelines" (last updated February 27, 2023)

text: Keep medicines in a dry, cool place, or as it says on the label. Only keep your medicine in the refrigerator if your doctor or pharmacist tells you to.

source.title: Staying Safe When You Take Several Medicines (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Medicine Storage Guidance" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Store all medicines and natural health products as directed and keep them out of reach of children. If small children are around, store medicines in containers or cabinets with childproof safety latches.

source.title: Your Home Health Centre (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Safety Precautions" (last updated February 27, 2023)

text: Verify expiration dates on your medicines regularly. When a medicine is expired or no longer needed, ask your pharmacist about safe disposal options.

source.title: Keeping Track of Medicines (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Know Your Medicines" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Most pharmacies offer a take-back program or a drop-off location for unused or expired medicines. Local municipalities and police offices may also provide these services. You can return your medicines through one of these programs at no charge.

source.title: How to Get Rid of Medicines (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Use Take-Back Programs" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: When no take-back program is available, remove the medicine from its original container and scratch out any personal information on the label to protect your identity. Mix the medicine with an unpalatable substance such as cat litter, sawdust, or coffee grounds—do not crush tablets or capsules. Place the mixture in a sealed plastic bag or can, then dispose of it in your regular household trash.

source.title: How to Get Rid of Medicines (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Trash Disposal Steps" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Health Canada recommends against flushing any medicine down the toilet or sink. Used opioid patches should be folded sticky-side together and delivered immediately to a pharmacy—do not throw them in the trash.

source.title: How to Get Rid of Medicines (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Do Not Flush Medications" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Wash your hands thoroughly before and after applying eye drops. Keep the dropper clean and do not let it touch the eye, eyelid, lashes, or any surface in order to prevent contamination. Never reuse a dropper that has contacted the eye, and do not share eye drops with another person.

source.title: Eye Problems: Using Eyedrops and Eye Ointment (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Safety Tips" (last updated October 12, 2022)

text: For older children and adults, tilt your head backward and pull down the lower eyelid to form a pouch. Gently squeeze the dropper to put one eyedrop in the pouch. Close the eye for 30 to 60 seconds to let the drop absorb. If your doctor told you to put in more than one drop, wait at least 5 minutes between drops.

source.title: Eye Problems: Using Eyedrops and Eye Ointment (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Using Eyedrops" (last updated October 12, 2022)

text: Tilt your head back and pull the lower eyelid down with one or two fingers to create a pouch. Gently squeeze the dropper to place a drop in the pouch. Keep your eye closed for 30 to 60 seconds to allow absorption. If the dropper contacts the eye area, obtain a replacement bottle or dropper from a pharmacy.

source.title: Eyedrops (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Instructions for Using Eyedrops" (last updated October 12, 2022)

text: A metered-dose inhaler delivers a measured amount of medicine as a liquid mist into the lungs, working faster than pills and requiring less medicine overall. A spacer is a chamber that you attach to the inhaler to hold the medicine before you breathe it in, making inhalation easier. If you use a corticosteroid inhaler, rinse your mouth after use without swallowing.

source.title: Breathing Problems: Using a Metered-Dose Inhaler (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Overview" (last updated November 14, 2022)

text: Shake the inhaler and remove the cap. Insert the mouthpiece into the spacer and remove the spacer cap. Hold the inhaler upright with the mouthpiece at the bottom. Stand or sit up straight, tilt your head back slightly, and breathe out slowly and completely. Place the spacer mouthpiece in your mouth. Press down on the inhaler to spray one puff of medicine into the spacer and then start breathing in slowly and deeply—press first, then breathe in. Hold your breath for 10 seconds to give the medicine time to settle in your lungs, then breathe out slowly. For additional doses, wait 30 to 60 seconds to allow the inhaler valve to refill.

source.title: Using a Metered-Dose Inhaler with a Spacer (Adult) (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Step-by-Step Instructions" (last updated November 14, 2022)

text: All medicines can cause side effects, though many people do not have them and minor side effects sometimes go away on their own. Contact emergency services immediately if you experience trouble breathing, hives, swelling of the face or throat, or loss of consciousness after taking a medication. For other side effects, discussing with your doctor or pharmacist may lead to dose adjustments or alternative medications.

source.title: Dealing With Medicine Side Effects and Interactions (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Side Effects" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Before starting any new medicine, ask your doctor or pharmacist about possible side effects you are likely to experience, when they may appear, whether they typically resolve on their own, any preventive measures such as timing or food interactions, and what monitoring may be required. Maintain an updated master list of all medicines including prescription drugs, over-the-counter medicines, and natural health products such as vitamins, minerals, and herbs.

source.title: Dealing With Medicine Side Effects and Interactions (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Prevention Strategies" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Medicine interactions can occur when one medicine makes another stronger or weaker, when side effects from one drug interfere with another's function, or when medicines worsen existing health conditions. Interactions can also happen with over-the-counter medicines, natural health products, and certain foods or beverages. Ask your pharmacist to run your full medicine list through a drug interaction checker, and use one consistent pharmacy so your pharmacist can see all your medicines at once.

source.title: Dealing With Medicine Side Effects and Interactions (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Medicine Interactions" (last updated March 1, 2023)

text: Use a digital thermometer for accurate readings. Fever strips and pacifier thermometers do not give accurate temperatures and should be avoided. Mercury thermometers should not be used due to toxicity risks. Normal armpit temperature ranges from 36.5°C to 37.2°C; normal oral temperature ranges from 35.5°C to 37.2°C; normal ear temperature ranges from 35.8°C to 38°C.

source.title: How to take a temperature: Children and adults (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #99, section "What type of thermometer should I use?" (last updated April 23, 2025)

text: For the armpit method, position the thermometer tip in the center of the armpit and hold the arm firmly against the body for about one minute until the thermometer beeps, then read the result. For the oral method (suitable for children aged 5 and older), place the thermometer tip under the tongue, off to one side at the base, keep the mouth closed, and wait about one minute for the beep signal before removing and reading.

source.title: How to take a temperature: Children and adults (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #99, section "How to Take a Child's Temperature" (last updated April 23, 2025)

text: For the ear method (suitable for children aged 2 and older), use a fresh probe tip each time, follow the manufacturer's instructions precisely, press and hold the button for one second, then remove and read the temperature. Seek medical attention if a fever lasts longer than 5 days, or is accompanied by reduced fluid intake, low energy, rash, cough, vomiting, diarrhea, breathing difficulties, or a stiff neck.

source.title: How to take a temperature: Children and adults (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #99, section "When to Contact a Healthcare Provider" (last updated April 23, 2025)

text: Before taking your blood pressure, do not drink caffeine, smoke, or exercise for at least 30 minutes. Empty your bladder before the test. Sit with your back straight, with both feet flat on the floor, and rest quietly for at least 5 minutes before taking a reading. Do not move, talk, or text while you take your blood pressure.

source.title: High Blood Pressure: Checking Your Blood Pressure at Home (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "How to take your blood pressure at home" (last updated September 7, 2022)

text: Sit with your arm slightly bent and resting comfortably on a table so that your upper arm is at the same level as your heart. Use the same arm each time you take your blood pressure. Wrap the blood pressure cuff around your bare upper arm, with the lower edge about 2.5 centimetres above the bend of your elbow. Press the start button, allow the cuff to deflate, then record the displayed reading with the date and time. Wait one minute and repeat the measurement.

source.title: High Blood Pressure: Checking Your Blood Pressure at Home (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Using a blood pressure monitor at home" (last updated September 7, 2022)

text: Water keeps every part of your body working properly, helping maintain body temperature, prevent kidney stones, and support waste elimination. A common recommendation is to drink six to eight 250 mL glasses of water or other fluid every day, though individual needs vary based on health status, exercise level, and climate.

source.title: Drinking Enough Water (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Why is it important to drink enough water?" (last updated September 12, 2023)

text: Signs that you are drinking enough fluid include urinating every few hours during the day, feeling well, and not experiencing frequent thirst. Practical ways to get more water each day include drinking water upon waking before coffee or tea, keeping water accessible at your workspace, and adding sugarless flavoring or citrus to plain water if preferred. Water-rich foods such as watermelon and lettuce also help maintain fluid intake.

source.title: Drinking Enough Water (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "What are some easy ways to get more water during the day?" (last updated September 12, 2023)

text: Storing food promptly and correctly can help prevent foodborne illness. Set your refrigerator at or below 4°C (40°F) and your freezer at or below -18°C (0°F). Refrigerate or freeze meat, poultry, eggs, fish, shellfish, and leftovers within 2 hours, or within 1 hour if the temperature is above 32°C (90°F).

source.title: Food Safety: Storing (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Overview"

text: Never store cooked or ready-to-eat food below raw food in the refrigerator. Do not pack your refrigerator — cool air must be able to circulate to keep food at a safe temperature. Always store food in leak-proof, clean containers with tight-fitting lids.

source.title: Food Safety: Storing (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Storage Practices"

text: Divide large amounts of leftovers into shallow containers for quicker cooling before placing them in the refrigerator. Use refrigerated leftovers within 3 to 4 days. Fresh poultry, fish, and ground meats should be used within 2 days; beef, veal, lamb, and pork within 3 to 5 days.

source.title: Food Safety: Storing (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Leftover Management"

text: Wash your hands with running water and soap, scrubbing for at least 20 seconds. Pay attention to your wrists, the backs of your hands, the spaces between your fingers, and under your nails. Wash your hands after using the bathroom, coughing or sneezing, handling raw meat, poultry, fish, shellfish, or eggs, and after touching soiled items.

source.title: Food Safety: Preparing (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Handwashing Instructions"

text: Keep raw meat, poultry, eggs, fish, and shellfish away from other foods, surfaces, utensils, and serving plates. Do not rinse raw meat and poultry — rinsing makes it more likely that bacteria will spread to kitchen surfaces and ready-to-eat foods. Use separate cutting boards: one for fresh produce and another for raw meat, poultry, and seafood.

source.title: Food Safety: Preparing (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Food Preparation Guidance"

text: Thaw frozen foods in the refrigerator or in the microwave — never on the countertop. Cook food immediately after thawing in the microwave. Marinate foods in the refrigerator in a covered dish. Wash raw fruits and vegetables under running water before eating or cooking them.

source.title: Food Safety: Preparing (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Food Preparation Guidance"

text: Use a clean meat thermometer to verify that meat, poultry, and egg dishes reach safe internal temperatures. Insert the thermometer all the way to the middle of the food and avoid touching any bones. Cooking foods to a safe temperature is the most reliable way to kill harmful bacteria.

source.title: Food Safety: Cooking (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Using Thermometers and Reheating"

text: Cook eggs until the whites and yolks are firm. Cook fish and shellfish until it is opaque and flakes easily with a fork. Do not eat undercooked hamburger — it is the primary source of E. coli infection. Avoid raw or partially cooked eggs, unpasteurized milk and cheeses, and unpasteurized juices.

source.title: Food Safety: Cooking (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Specific Foods"

text: Reheat other leftovers to at least 74°C (165°F). Bring sauces, gravies, and soups to a boil when reheating. When using a microwave oven, cover the food container and turn or stir the food to make sure it is heated evenly throughout. Rotate the dish by hand once or twice if the microwave does not have a turntable.

source.title: Food Safety: Cooking (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Microwave Safety"

text: Install solid handrails on both sides of indoor stairs and outdoor steps. Ensure stairs are well-lit with light switches at the top and bottom. Apply a non-skid surface on outdoor steps. Remove carpets or rugs that can be a tripping hazard, and maintain clear pathways free of clutter.

source.title: Seniors' falls can be prevented (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #78, section "Home Safety" (last updated July 31, 2024)

text: Adults over 65 should engage in at least 150 minutes of moderate to vigorous aerobic physical activities per week. Resistance training and balance exercises such as tai chi, done twice weekly, can improve strength and reduce fall risk. Falls are predictable and preventable — they are not a normal part of aging.

source.title: Seniors' falls can be prevented (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #78, section "Physical Activity" (last updated July 31, 2024)

text: Every year, thousands of older adults fall and hurt themselves, and those who have fallen once are 2 to 3 times more likely to fall again. Rearrange kitchen cabinets so that items used most often are within easy reach without climbing. Make sure step stools have a handrail and non-skid feet. Install a night-light in the hallway and between the bedroom and bathroom.

source.title: Checklist for Preventing Falls at Home (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Kitchen"

text: Put a rubber mat that won't slip, or self-stick non-slip strips, on the floor of the tub or shower. Have a carpenter install grab bars inside the tub and next to the toilet. These supports give you something secure to hold when getting in and out of the tub or shower and when sitting down and standing up.

source.title: Checklist for Preventing Falls at Home (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Bathroom Safety Checklist"

text: Install grab bars by the toilet, in the bathtub, and in shower areas. Use bathroom aids such as bath seats and toilet risers to make bathing and using the toilet safer and easier. These modifications are among the most effective steps to prevent falls in the bathroom.

source.title: Seniors' falls can be prevented (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #78, section "Home Safety" (last updated July 31, 2024)

text: Heat-related illness occurs when your body is gaining heat faster than it can cool itself down. In severe cases this can progress to heat stroke, a life-threatening medical emergency. Stay indoors in air-conditioned buildings, or take a cool bath or shower. Drink plenty of fluids — drink extra water even before you feel thirsty.

source.title: Heat-related illness (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #35, section "Overview" (last updated June 5, 2026)

text: Schedule outdoor activity before 10 am or after 4 pm to avoid the hottest part of the day. Wear lightweight, light-coloured, loose-fitting clothing and a wide-brimmed hat. Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen with SPF 30 or higher on exposed skin.

source.title: Heat-related illness (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #35, section "Prevention Strategies" (last updated June 5, 2026)

text: Never leave children, dependent adults, or pets alone in a parked car — temperatures inside a vehicle can rise to dangerous levels within minutes. High-risk groups include seniors over 65, socially isolated individuals, infants under 4, and people with chronic or mental illnesses. Call 9-1-1 immediately if you suspect someone has heat stroke.

source.title: Heat-related illness (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC File #35, section "High-Risk Groups" (last updated June 5, 2026)

text: Avoid outdoor activity in extreme cold, and take the wind chill into account when deciding whether it is safe to go outside. Choose well-lit times for outdoor exercise and use shoe grippers to prevent slipping on icy surfaces. When outdoor activity is not safe, consider indoor alternatives such as mall walking or home exercise.

source.title: Quick Tips: Staying Active in Cold Weather (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Safety Considerations"

text: Dress in layers using wool or synthetic materials such as polyester or nylon — not cotton. Wear a warm hat and a waterproof outer layer. Choose mittens rather than gloves so your fingers can stay together for warmth. Wear properly fitting wool socks with waterproof shoes.

source.title: Quick Tips: Staying Active in Cold Weather (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Appropriate Clothing"

text: Cold injury occurs readily when you are outside in wet, windy, or cold weather. Wet or inadequate clothing is a major risk factor. Remove wet clothing as soon as possible and change into moisture-wicking fabrics such as wool, polyester, or nylon. Seek shelter immediately if you experience numbness, stiffness, shivering, or poor judgment — these can be early signs of cold injury or hypothermia.

source.title: Cold Temperature Exposure (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Self-Care Guidance"

text: Purchase and install carbon monoxide detectors on each level of your home and near sleeping areas. Have your heating system inspected each year. Monitor appliances, chimneys, and vents for visible soot, rust, stains, blockage, or corrosion.

source.title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning: Protecting Your Home (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Detection and Inspection"

text: Ensure proper ventilation when using appliances to allow gas to escape from enclosed areas. Do not close fireplace dampers before fires are completely extinguished.

source.title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning: Protecting Your Home (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Appliance Safety"

text: Do not use kerosene or propane heaters in enclosed spaces. Do not use a gas kitchen oven to heat your home. Refrain from burning charcoal or operating grills indoors. Keep power generators and pressure washers away from windows and doors to prevent carbon monoxide from entering the home.

source.title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning: Protecting Your Home (HealthLink BC)
source.citation: HealthLinkBC, section "Prohibited Indoor Activities"
